verses.flow: Paul and Silvanus and Timothy, to the assembly of the Thessalonians in God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ: Grace to you and peace.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ekklēsia" (ἐκκλησία, Strong's G1577) denotes a called-out assembly, not a building; its LXX background translates Hebrew "qahal," the congregation of Israel. The phrase "in God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" is remarkable, placing Father and Son in a single prepositional phrase as the sphere in which the assembly exists — a striking co-ordination of divine persons. HISTORICAL: Thessalonica was the capital of the Roman province of Macedonia, a major port city on the Via Egnatia. This letter is widely regarded as the earliest Pauline epistle, written c. AD 49–51 from Corinth.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: We give thanks to God always concerning all of you, making mention upon our prayers unceasingly,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: remembering your work of faith and labor of love and endurance of hope in our Lord Jesus Christ, before our God and Father,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The triad "work of faith, labor of love, endurance of hope" maps onto the great Pauline virtues of faith, love, and hope (1 Cor 13:13). Each virtue is linked to active expression — faith produces work (ergon), love produces toilsome labor (kopos), and hope produces steadfast endurance (hypomonē). GREEK: "Hypomonē" (ὑπομονή, G5281) denotes not passive waiting but active, resolute endurance under pressure. The phrase "before our God and Father" (emprosthen tou theou) evokes divine-court language — their lives are lived in the presence of the divine tribunal.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine
verb perfect passive participle plural masculine

verses.flow: knowing, brothers beloved by God, your election,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Eklogē" (ἐκλογή, G1589) — election, choosing out — is here grounded not in human merit but in divine love: "beloved by God." Paul's confidence in their election is confirmed by the evidence of the Spirit's power (v. 5). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The concept of God's election of a people echoes the Deuteronomy 32:8–9 worldview where YHWH chose Israel as his own portion from among the nations; now this election extends to Gentile believers in Thessalonica.

verses.flow: that our gospel did not come to you in word only, but also in power and in the Holy Spirit and in much full assurance, even as you know what manner of men we became among you for your sake.
verses.note: GREEK: "Plērophoria" (πληροφορία, G4136) means full assurance or complete conviction — a rare term in the NT (cf. Col 2:2; Heb 6:11; 10:22). THEOLOGICAL: The triadic "power, Holy Spirit, full assurance" describes the gospel's arrival not as mere rhetoric (logos monon) but as a divine incursion. This distinguishes apostolic preaching from Greco-Roman sophistic oratory, grounding its efficacy in the Spirit rather than human eloquence.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And you became imitators of us and of the Lord, having received the word in much affliction with joy of the Holy Spirit,

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: so that you became a pattern to all those believing in Macedonia and in Achaia.
For from you the word of the Lord has sounded forth, not only in Macedonia and Achaia, but in every place your faith toward God has gone out, so that we have no need to speak anything.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: For they themselves report concerning us what manner of entrance we had to you, and how you turned to God from the idols, to serve a living and true God,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Thessalonians' conversion is described in classic covenantal terms: turning (epistrephō) from idols to the living and true God. This echoes prophetic calls in the OT (cf. Isa 44:9–20; Jer 10:10). GREEK: "Zōnti kai alēthinō" (ζῶντι καὶ ἀληθινῷ) — "living and true" — contrasts the God of Israel with the dead and false gods of the nations. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The language of turning from idols to serve the living God implicitly invokes the Deuteronomy 32 framework in which the nations were given over to lesser elohim (cf. Deut 4:19–20; Ps 96:5 LXX), while Israel's God alone is the true and living sovereign.

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative singular masculine
verb present middle participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: and to await his Son from the heavens, whom he raised from the dead — Jesus, the one delivering us from the wrath that is coming.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse encapsulates Pauline eschatology: the awaiting (anamenō, a NT hapax legomenon) of Christ's return from heaven, his resurrection as the ground of that hope, and deliverance from coming wrath. GREEK: "Anamenein" (ἀναμένειν, G362) appears only here in the NT — it conveys patient, expectant waiting and underscores the forward-looking posture of early Christian faith. TYPOLOGY: "The one delivering us from the wrath that is coming" (ton rhyomenon) uses a present participle, portraying Jesus as the continually active deliverer — echoing the divine rescuer motif from the Exodus and the Psalms (cf. Ps 18:2 LXX; Dan 6:27 LXX). The "coming wrath" (orgē erchomenē) connects to OT Day-of-the-Lord imagery (Joel 2:31; Zeph 1:15) and anticipates the fuller eschatological teaching in chapters 4–5.

verses.flow: For you yourselves know, brothers, our coming in unto you, that it has not been empty,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: but having suffered beforehand and having been shamefully treated, as you know, in Philippi, we were bold in our God to speak unto you the gospel of God in much conflict.
verses.note: GREEK: προπαθόντες (propathontes, from propáschō) means "having suffered beforehand," referring to the events in Philippi (Acts 16:19-24). ἐπαρρησιασάμεθα (eparrēsiasametha, from parrhēsiázomai, Strong's G3955) means "we spoke boldly/freely," a term denoting courageous public proclamation despite opposition. The phrase ἐν πολλῷ ἀγῶνι ("in much conflict") uses agōn (G73), an athletic contest term suggesting intense spiritual struggle.

verses.flow: For our exhortation is not out of error, nor out of uncleanness, nor in deceit,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: but just as we have been approved by God to be entrusted with the gospel, so we speak—not as pleasing men, but God, the One who examines our hearts.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul establishes that apostolic authority rests on divine approval (δεδοκιμάσμεθα, dedokimasmetha—"we have been tested and approved," perfect passive). The same verb δοκιμάζω (dokimazō, G1381) is used for God's testing of hearts, creating a deliberate parallel: the God who approved us is the God who continually examines (present participle) our hearts. Ministry accountability is ultimately vertical, not horizontal.

verses.flow: For neither at any time did we come with a word of flattery, as you know, nor with a pretext for covetousness—God is witness—
verses.note: GREEK: κολακεία (kolakeía, G2850) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "flattery." It was a term of moral censure in Greco-Roman rhetoric, denoting speech designed to manipulate rather than edify. πρόφασις (próphasis, G4392) means "pretext" or "cloak"—an outward cover hiding the true motive of πλεονεξία (pleonexía, G4124), "greedy desire for more."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: nor seeking glory from men, neither from you nor from others,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: though being able to be a burden as apostles of Christ; but we became gentle in the midst of you, as a nursing mother cherishes her own children.
verses.note: GREEK: There is a significant textual variant here: ἤπιοι (ēpioi, "gentle") versus νήπιοι (nēpioi, "infants"). The difference is a single letter (nu). Most critical editions read ἤπιοι. τροφός (trophós, G5162) refers specifically to a nursing mother or wet-nurse, not merely a caretaker. θάλπω (thálpō, G2282) means "to warm, cherish, tenderly care for" (cf. Eph 5:29), conveying intimate nurturing.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: So, longing for you, we were well-pleased to share with you not only the gospel of God but also our own souls, because you had become beloved to us.
verses.note: GREEK: ὁμειρόμενοι (homeiromenoi) is a rare word, possibly from ὅμειρος, expressing deep affectionate longing. Some connect it to a root meaning "to yearn over." Paul's willingness to share not only the gospel but τὰς ἑαυτῶν ψυχάς ("our own souls/lives") demonstrates that authentic gospel ministry is inseparable from self-giving love.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For you remember, brothers, our labor and toil: working night and day, so as not to burden any of you, we proclaimed unto you the gospel of God.

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: You are witnesses, and God also, how devoutly and righteously and blamelessly we conducted ourselves toward you who believe,
just as you know how, as a father his own children, each one of you,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: we were exhorting you and encouraging and charging you to walk worthily of God, the One calling you into His own kingdom and glory.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The triad of pastoral action—παρακαλοῦντες (exhorting), παραμυθούμενοι (encouraging), μαρτυρόμενοι (solemnly charging)—describes comprehensive spiritual fathering. The goal is walking ἀξίως τοῦ θεοῦ ("worthily of God"), who is described as the One presently calling (καλοῦντος, present participle) believers into His own βασιλείαν καὶ δόξαν ("kingdom and glory"), indicating both present and eschatological realities of God's reign.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: And on account of this we also give thanks to God unceasingly, that having received the word of hearing from us—the word of God—you accepted it not as a word of men, but as it truly is, the word of God, which also is at work in you who believe.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul distinguishes between παραλαβόντες (receiving outwardly, the act of hearing) and ἐδέξασθε (accepting inwardly, welcoming with faith). The word of God is described as ἐνεργεῖται (energeitai, G1754)—"is at work, is operative"—a middle/passive form indicating divine power actively working within believers. This verse is a key text for the doctrine of Scripture's divine origin and efficacy (cf. Heb 4:12).

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural feminine

verses.flow: For you became imitators, brothers, of the assemblies of God which are in Judea in Christ Jesus, because you also suffered the same things from your own countrymen, just as they also from the Jews,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: συμφυλετῶν (symphyletōn, G4853) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "fellow-countrymen" or "those of the same tribe." The Thessalonian believers suffered persecution from pagan Gentile neighbors, mirroring the Judean churches' suffering from Jewish opponents. TYPOLOGY: This pattern of the people of God suffering from their own kinsmen echoes the prophetic tradition of Israel persecuting its own prophets (cf. Matt 23:31-37).

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive plural masculine
verb aorist active participle genitive plural masculine
verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: who both killed the Lord Jesus and the prophets, and drove us out, and are not pleasing to God, and are contrary to all men,

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: hindering us from speaking to the nations that they might be saved, so as to fill up their sins always. But the wrath has come upon them unto the end.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: εἰς τὸ ἀναπληρῶσαι αὐτῶν τὰς ἁμαρτίας πάντοτε ("so as to fill up their sins always") echoes Genesis 15:16 where the iniquity of the Amorites must be "filled up." ἔφθασεν (ephthasen, aorist of phthánō, G5348) indicates the wrath "has arrived" or "has overtaken"—a prophetic aorist pointing to divinely determined judgment. εἰς τέλος may mean "unto the end," "completely," or "finally."

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But we, brothers, having been orphaned from you for a season of an hour—in face, not in heart—were all the more eager with much desire to see your face.
For we desired to come unto you—I, Paul, indeed, both once and twice—and Satan hindered us.
For what is our hope, or joy, or crown of boasting—is it not indeed also you—before our Lord Jesus at His coming?
verses.note: GREEK: στέφανος καυχήσεως (stephanos kauchēseōs) is the "crown of boasting"—stephanos (G4735) being the victor's wreath, not the royal diadēma. THEOLOGICAL: παρουσία (parousía, G3952) is used here for the coming/arrival of the Lord Jesus—a term used in Hellenistic Greek for the official visit of a king or emperor. Paul identifies the Thessalonians themselves as his eschatological reward at Christ's return, binding ecclesiology to eschatology.

verses.flow: For you are our glory and joy.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Wherefore, no longer bearing it, we were well-pleased to be left behind in Athens alone,
and we sent Timothy, our brother and fellow-worker of God in the gospel of Christ, to establish you and to encourage you concerning your faith,
verses.note: GREEK: συνεργὸν τοῦ θεοῦ (synergon tou theou, "fellow-worker of God") is a striking title for Timothy, designating him as a co-laborer in divine service. Some manuscripts read διάκονον τοῦ θεοῦ ("servant of God") or συνεργὸν ἡμῶν ("our fellow-worker"), likely scribal softening of the bold original. στηρίξαι (stērixai, Strong's G4741) means "to fix firmly, establish"—Paul's chief concern is the stabilization of new believers under pressure. παρακαλέσαι (parakalesai, G3870) carries the dual sense of "encourage" and "exhort."

verses.flow: that no one be moved by these afflictions; for you yourselves know that unto this we are appointed.
verses.note: GREEK: σαίνεσθαι (sainesthai, G4525) is a rare word, a NT hapax legomenon. Its basic meaning is "to wag the tail" (of a dog), hence "to fawn upon" or "to beguile," but here in the passive it means "to be shaken, disturbed, moved." THEOLOGICAL: Paul teaches that affliction (θλῖψις, thlipsis) is not incidental but appointed (κείμεθα, keimetha—"we are set, placed, destined"). This echoes the pattern that suffering is integral to the messianic community's calling (Acts 14:22; Phil 1:29).

verses.flow: For indeed, when we were with you, we were telling you beforehand that we are about to suffer affliction, even as it also came to pass, and you know.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: On account of this, I also, no longer bearing it, sent to know your faith, lest somehow the tempter had tempted you and our labor should become in vain.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: ὁ πειράζων (ho peirazōn, "the tempter," G3985) is a title for Satan as the adversary who tests believers to draw them away from faith (Matt 4:3). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The cosmic conflict framework is implicit—the tempter operates as an opposing spiritual agent seeking to render apostolic labor κενός (kenos, "empty, vain"). Paul's concern is not merely pastoral but reflects awareness of spiritual warfare against the ecclesia.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle genitive singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But just now, Timothy having come to us from you and having brought good tidings to us of your faith and your love, and that you have good remembrance of us always, longing to see us, even as we also you—
on account of this we were encouraged, brothers, over you, in all our distress and affliction, through your faith;
for now we live, if you stand firm in the Lord.
verses.note: GREEK: στήκετε (stēkete, G4739) is a late Greek form meaning "to stand firm, hold one's ground," derived from the perfect stem of ἵστημι. It carries a military connotation of maintaining one's position. THEOLOGICAL: Paul's declaration "now we live" (νῦν ζῶμεν) reveals the depth of apostolic solidarity—his own vitality and spiritual life are bound up with the perseverance of his converts in the Lord.

verses.flow: For what thanksgiving are we able to render to God concerning you, for all the joy with which we rejoice on account of you before our God,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: night and day praying beyond all measure to see your face and to complete the things lacking in your faith?
Now may our God and Father himself, and our Lord Jesus, direct our way to you;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The prayer is addressed jointly to "our God and Father himself, and our Lord Jesus," with the singular optative κατευθύναι (kateuthynai, "may he direct") governing both subjects. This grammatical coordination of God the Father and the Lord Jesus under a single verb is a significant indicator of early high Christology, placing Jesus in a position of divine agency alongside the Father. STRUCTURE: Verses 11-13 form a prayer-wish that closes the first half of the epistle, transitioning from narrative to petition.

verses.flow: and may the Lord cause you to increase and to abound in love toward one another and toward all, even as we also toward you,
so as to establish your hearts blameless in holiness before our God and Father, at the coming of our Lord Jesus with all his holy ones.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: παρουσία (parousia, G3952) denotes the "coming, arrival, presence" of the Lord Jesus—a term used in the Hellenistic world for the royal visitation of a king. This is the eschatological anchor of the entire chapter: perseverance in holiness is oriented toward the Lord's return. DIVINE_COUNCIL: "With all his holy ones" (μετὰ πάντων τῶν ἁγίων αὐτοῦ) echoes Zechariah 14:5 and Deuteronomy 33:2 (LXX), where God comes with his holy ones—angelic hosts of the divine council. Paul applies this theophanic imagery directly to Jesus, depicting his parousia as a divine-council advent (cf. 2 Thess 1:7; Jude 14).

verses.flow: Finally then, brothers, we ask you and exhort you in the Lord Jesus, that just as you received from us how it is necessary for you to walk and to please God—just as indeed you are walking—that you abound yet more.
For you know what instructions we gave to you through the Lord Jesus.
For this is the will of God, your sanctification: that you abstain from sexual immorality,
verses.note: GREEK: "Sanctification" (ἁγιασμός, hagiasmos, Strong's G38) denotes the process of being set apart as holy. "Sexual immorality" (πορνεία, porneia, Strong's G4202) covers all sexual activity outside the covenant of marriage, a term with broad semantic range in both Jewish and Greco-Roman moral discourse. THEOLOGICAL: Paul grounds ethical behavior not in social convention but in the express will (θέλημα) of God Himself, making holiness a divine imperative rather than a human aspiration.

verses.flow: that each one of you know how to acquire his own vessel in sanctification and honor,
verses.note: GREEK: "Vessel" (σκεῦος, skeuos, Strong's G4632) is debated—it may refer to one's own body (cf. 2 Cor 4:7) or to one's wife (cf. 1 Pet 3:7). "Acquire" or "possess" (κτᾶσθαι, ktasthai) suggests ongoing mastery and control. HISTORICAL: In Greco-Roman Thessalonica, sexual license was culturally normative; Paul's instruction would have been radically countercultural.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: not in the passion of desire, even as also the Gentiles who do not know God,
that no one transgress and defraud his brother in the matter, because the Lord is an avenger concerning all these things, just as also we told you beforehand and solemnly testified.
verses.note: GREEK: "Transgress" (ὑπερβαίνειν, hyperbainein, Strong's G5233) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "to go beyond" or "to overstep." "Defraud" (πλεονεκτεῖν, pleonektein) means to take advantage of or exploit. THEOLOGICAL: The Lord (κύριος) is identified as the divine avenger (ἔκδικος), echoing the role of YHWH as judge and vindicator of covenant justice (cf. Deut 32:35; Ps 94:1).

verses.flow: For God did not call us unto uncleanness, but in sanctification.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore, the one who rejects this rejects not man but God, the one who also gives His Holy Spirit unto you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Rejection of apostolic teaching is equated with rejection of God Himself, establishing the authoritative nature of Spirit-inspired instruction. The present participle "the one giving" (διδόντα, didonta) indicates the ongoing gift of the Holy Spirit, not merely a past event. DIVINE_COUNCIL: God's own Spirit (τὸ πνεῦμα αὐτοῦ τὸ ἅγιον) is the agent of sanctification dwelling within believers, marking them as belonging to the divine realm.

verses.flow: Now concerning brotherly love, you have no need for anyone to write to you, for you yourselves are taught by God to love one another.
verses.note: GREEK: "Taught by God" (θεοδίδακτοι, theodidaktoi, Strong's G2312) is a NT hapax legomenon—a compound of θεός (God) and διδακτός (taught). TYPOLOGY: This echoes the prophetic promise of Isa 54:13 (LXX) and Jer 31:33-34, where God Himself inscribes His instruction upon the hearts of His people in the new covenant era. Jesus cites this tradition in John 6:45.

verses.flow: For indeed you do this toward all the brothers who are in all of Macedonia. But we exhort you, brothers, to abound yet more,
and to aspire earnestly to live quietly, and to attend to your own affairs, and to work with your own hands, just as we instructed you,
so that you may walk properly toward those outside, and may have need of nothing.

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But we do not want you to be ignorant, brothers, concerning those who have fallen asleep, so that you may not grieve as also the rest who have no hope.
verses.note: GREEK: "Those who have fallen asleep" (τῶν κοιμωμένων, tōn koimōmenōn) uses the common early Christian metaphor of death as sleep, implying the expectation of awakening at resurrection. HISTORICAL: The Thessalonian believers were evidently distressed that some among them had died before Christ's return. Paul writes to correct a misunderstanding about the fate of deceased believers. THEOLOGICAL: Christian grief is not forbidden but is distinguished from the hopeless grief of those without the resurrection hope (cf. Eph 2:12).

words.m: verb aorist passive participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: For if we believe that Jesus died and rose again, so also God will bring with Him those who have fallen asleep through Jesus.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The creedal formula "Jesus died and rose again" (ἀπέθανεν καὶ ἀνέστη) forms the ground of eschatological hope: Christ's bodily resurrection guarantees the future resurrection of those who belong to Him. The phrase "through Jesus" (διὰ τοῦ Ἰησοῦ) may modify either "fallen asleep" (died in union with Jesus) or "will bring" (God will bring them through Jesus). TYPOLOGY: As the firstfruits of resurrection (1 Cor 15:20), Christ's rising prefigures and secures the harvest of all who sleep in Him.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist passive participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: For this we say to you by a word of the Lord, that we who are living, who remain unto the coming of the Lord, shall by no means precede those who have fallen asleep.
For the Lord Himself shall descend from heaven with a command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the trumpet of God, and the dead in Christ shall rise first.
verses.note: GREEK: "Command" (κέλευσμα, keleusma, Strong's G2752) is a NT hapax legomenon denoting a shout of authority, as a military commander or charioteer. "Archangel" (ἀρχαγγέλου, archangelou) appears only here and in Jude 9 in the NT. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The scene depicts a royal descent with heavenly host fanfare—the command, the archangelic voice, and the divine trumpet together signal the arrival of the King with His heavenly court (cf. Exod 19:16; Zech 9:14; Matt 24:31). TYPOLOGY: The trumpet of God echoes Sinai theophany imagery, now fulfilled in Christ's Parousia.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Then we who are living, who remain, shall be caught up together with them in the clouds, unto a meeting of the Lord in the air; and so we shall always be with the Lord.
verses.note: GREEK: "Caught up" (ἁρπαγησόμεθα, harpagēsometha, from ἁρπάζω, harpazō, Strong's G726) denotes a sudden, forceful seizing—the Latin Vulgate renders this "rapiemur," from which the term "rapture" derives. "Meeting" (ἀπάντησιν, apantēsin, Strong's G529) is a technical term in Hellenistic Greek for the civic custom of citizens going out to meet a arriving dignitary and escorting him back into the city. HISTORICAL: The ἀπάντησις imagery suggests not a departure from earth but an escort of the returning King—believers meet the Lord in the air to accompany Him as He descends to reign. THEOLOGICAL: The climactic promise "and so we shall always be with the Lord" (πάντοτε σὺν κυρίῳ ἐσόμεθα) is the ultimate hope—eternal presence with Christ, not a location, is the substance of salvation.

verses.flow: Therefore comfort one another with these words.
But concerning the times and the seasons, brothers, you have no need for it to be written to you,
for you yourselves know accurately that the day of the Lord so comes as a thief in the night.
verses.note: GREEK: "Akribōs" (ἀκριβῶς, accurately/exactly) emphasizes the thoroughness of their prior instruction. "Day of the Lord" (ἡμέρα κυρίου) is a direct LXX phrase (cf. Joel 2:1; Amos 5:18; Zeph 1:14) now applied to the return of Christ, identifying Jesus as the Lord of the eschatological day. TYPOLOGY: The "thief in the night" imagery echoes Jesus' own teaching (Matt 24:43; Luke 12:39) and recurs in 2 Pet 3:10 and Rev 16:15.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular feminine

verses.flow: When they say, "Peace and security," then sudden destruction comes upon them, even as the birth-pang upon her who is with child, and they shall by no means escape.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ólethros" (ὄλεθρος, destruction) does not denote annihilation but ruinous loss and judgment (cf. 2 Thess 1:9; 1 Cor 5:5). "Aiphnídios" (αἰφνίδιος, sudden) is rare in the NT, appearing only here and Luke 21:34. TYPOLOGY: The birth-pang (ὠδίν) metaphor draws on OT prophetic imagery for the travail preceding divine intervention (Isa 13:8; Jer 6:24; Mic 4:9-10). The double negative "οὐ μή" with subjunctive expresses the strongest possible negation: escape is utterly impossible.

verses.flow: But you, brothers, are not in darkness, that the day should overtake you as a thief;
for you are all sons of light and sons of day. We are not of night nor of darkness.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Sons of light" (υἱοὶ φωτός) is a Semitic idiom denoting those who belong to the realm of light; cf. the identical phrase in the Dead Sea Scrolls (1QS III.13—IV.26, "War of the Sons of Light"). Paul shifts from second person ("you are") to first person ("we are not"), uniting himself with the community. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The light/darkness dualism reflects the cosmic conflict between God's realm and the dominion of darkness (Col 1:13; Eph 6:12).

verses.flow: So then, let us not sleep as the rest do, but let us be watchful and let us be sober.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For those who sleep, sleep at night, and those who are drunk, are drunk at night.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But we, being of the day, let us be sober, having put on the breastplate of faith and love, and as a helmet the hope of salvation.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The armor imagery draws on Isaiah 59:17 where YHWH himself puts on righteousness as a breastplate and a helmet of salvation—Paul applies the divine warrior's own armor to believers. This is developed more fully in Eph 6:13-17. GREEK: "Endysámenoi" (ἐνδυσάμενοι) is an aorist middle participle—"having clothed yourselves"—indicating decisive action. The triad of faith, love, and hope mirrors 1 Thess 1:3.

verses.flow: For God did not appoint us unto wrath, but unto the obtaining of salvation through our Lord Jesus Christ,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Étheto" (ἔθετο, from τίθημι) means "appointed" or "destined," indicating God's sovereign decree. The contrast is not between wrath and mere rescue but between wrath and "peripoíēsis" (περιποίησιν)—the active obtaining/acquisition of salvation. God's purpose for his people is positive possession, not merely escape from judgment. GREEK: "Peripoíēsis" (Strong's G4047) can mean both the act of acquiring and the thing acquired (cf. Eph 1:14; 2 Thess 2:14).

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: the one who died on behalf of us, so that whether we are awake or whether we sleep, we might live together with him.
Therefore encourage one another and build up one another, just as also you are doing.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine
verb present middle participle accusative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: And we ask you, brothers, to know those who labor among you and who lead you in the Lord and who admonish you,
and to esteem them beyond all measure in love on account of their work. Be at peace among yourselves.
And we exhort you, brothers: admonish the disorderly, comfort the fainthearted, hold fast to the weak, be patient toward all.
verses.note: GREEK: "Atáktous" (ἀτάκτους, the disorderly) derives from military language for soldiers who break rank (Strong's G813); in Thessalonica this likely refers to those who quit working in anticipation of the Lord's return (cf. 2 Thess 3:6-11). "Oligopsýchous" (ὀλιγοψύχους, fainthearted/small-souled) is a NT hapax legomenon, though it appears in the LXX (Isa 35:4; 54:6). HISTORICAL: Paul prescribes differentiated pastoral care—not one approach for all, but specific responses matched to specific spiritual conditions.

verses.flow: See that no one renders evil for evil to anyone, but always pursue the good toward one another and toward all.
Rejoice always.
Pray without ceasing.
In everything give thanks; for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus for you.
The Spirit do not quench.
verses.note: GREEK: "Sbénnyte" (σβέννυτε) means "quench" or "extinguish," as of a fire (Strong's G4570; cf. Matt 12:20; Eph 6:16). The present imperative with "mē" prohibits a continuing action: "stop quenching" or "do not keep quenching." THEOLOGICAL: "To pneûma" (τὸ πνεῦμα) here refers to the Holy Spirit and his active operations within the assembly, closely linked with the next command about prophecy. Suppression of the Spirit's gifts was evidently a temptation in the Thessalonian congregation.

verses.flow: Prophecies do not despise.
But test all things; hold fast the good.
From every form of evil abstain.
And may the God of peace himself sanctify you wholly, and may your spirit and soul and body be kept blameless at the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ.
verses.note: GREEK: "Holoteleis" (ὁλοτελεῖς) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "completely" or "wholly unto the end" (from ὅλος + τέλος). "Holóklēron" (ὁλόκληρον) means "entire, complete in every part," used of unblemished sacrificial animals in the LXX (Deut 27:6). THEOLOGICAL: The tripartite "spirit and soul and body" (πνεῦμα καὶ ψυχὴ καὶ σῶμα) expresses the totality of human existence rather than a strict ontological division. Paul's prayer is that the whole person be sanctified and preserved blameless at the "parousia" (παρουσία)—the royal appearing of the Lord Jesus Christ.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Faithful is the one who calls you, who also will do it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Pistós" (πιστός, faithful) placed emphatically first declares that the sanctification prayed for in v.23 rests not on human effort but on the character of God who calls. The one calling (ὁ καλῶν, present participle) indicates God's ongoing calling activity. "Who also will do it" (ὃς καὶ ποιήσει) is a profound statement of divine faithfulness guaranteeing the completion of salvation (cf. Phil 1:6; Rom 8:30).

verses.flow: Brothers, pray for us.
Greet all the brothers with a holy kiss.
I adjure you by the Lord that this letter be read to all the brothers.
verses.note: GREEK: "Enorkízō" (ἐνορκίζω, I adjure) is a NT hapax legomenon, a strengthened form of "horkízō" (to put under oath). This is the strongest possible charge Paul issues in any of his letters. HISTORICAL: The solemn adjuration that this letter be read to "all the brothers" indicates Paul's apostolic authority and suggests the letter functioned as Scripture read aloud in the liturgical assembly. This may also indicate concern that some factions might not hear the letter's contents.

verses.flow: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you.
